article.title: Musk Announces $25 Billion Terafab Chip Factory as Tesla, SpaceX, and xAI Pursue Vertical Integration in Semiconductors
article.summary: Elon Musk unveiled Terafab, a joint chip fabrication facility in Austin backed by Tesla, SpaceX, and xAI, targeting 2-nanometer process technology and one terawatt of annual computing output, with 80 percent of production earmarked for orbital AI satellites.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Elon Musk took the stage at Austin's defunct Seaholm Power Plant on March 21 to announce Terafab, a semiconductor fabrication facility jointly backed by Tesla, SpaceX, and xAI. The project, estimated at $20 to $25 billion, aims to produce one terawatt of computing output annually using 2-nanometer process technology, according to [Engadget](https://www.engadget.com/science/elon-musk-announces-terafab-project-he-claims-will-be-the-largest-chip-manufacturing-facility-ever-171718545.html). Musk described it as "the most epic chip building exercise in history by far."

The facility is planned for the North Campus of Giga Texas in Austin and would consolidate chip design, lithography, fabrication, memory production, advanced packaging, and testing under a single roof.

## Two Fabs, Two Chip Lines

Terafab will consist of two fabrication plants, each dedicated to a single chip design. The first will produce Tesla's fifth-generation AI inference chip, designated AI5, intended for Full Self-Driving software, Cybercab robotaxis, and Optimus humanoid robots. Small-batch production of AI5 is targeted for late 2026, with volume manufacturing projected for 2027, as [Electrek reported](https://electrek.co/2026/03/22/tesla-spacex-terafab-chip-factory-ai-desperation/).

The second fab will manufacture the D3, a radiation-hardened processor designed for orbital operations. Musk stated that 80 percent of Terafab's total compute output would be directed toward space-based applications, with only 20 percent allocated to terrestrial uses, according to [Fortune](https://fortune.com/2026/03/22/musk-terafab-chip-project-tesla-spacex-xai-space-data-center-satellite/).

## Orbital Ambitions

The space-focused allocation reflects Musk's broader strategy for orbital computing infrastructure. SpaceX has filed an FCC application to launch one million data center satellites, each approximately 170 meters in length and carrying 100 kilowatts of onboard power, [Engadget noted](https://www.engadget.com/science/elon-musk-announces-terafab-project-he-claims-will-be-the-largest-chip-manufacturing-facility-ever-171718545.html). Musk argued that solar irradiance in space is roughly five times greater than on Earth's surface, potentially making orbital compute cost-competitive within two to three years.

The orbital data center constellation would primarily serve xAI, which SpaceX acquired in an all-stock deal in February 2026. SpaceX is also planning an IPO later in 2026 at a potential valuation exceeding $1.75 trillion, with the satellite AI infrastructure positioned as a key revenue driver, according to [Fortune](https://fortune.com/2026/03/22/musk-terafab-chip-project-tesla-spacex-xai-space-data-center-satellite/).

## Production Targets and Scale

[Electrek reported](https://electrek.co/2026/03/22/tesla-spacex-terafab-chip-factory-ai-desperation/) that Terafab's stated capacity goals are ambitious: an initial target of 100,000 wafer starts per month, scaling to one million wafer starts per month at full capacity. That upper figure would represent approximately 70 percent of TSMC's current global output. Annual chip production is projected at 100 to 200 billion custom AI and memory chips.

Musk acknowledged existing supplier relationships during the event, stating: "We're very grateful to our existing supply chain, to Samsung, TSMC, Micron," but added that current global chip production meets only about 2 percent of what his companies will require. "We either build the Terafab or we don't have the chips. And we need the chips so we're going to build the Terafab," he said, as quoted by [Engadget](https://www.engadget.com/science/elon-musk-announces-terafab-project-he-claims-will-be-the-largest-chip-manufacturing-facility-ever-171718545.html).

## Funding and Timeline Gaps

Tesla's CFO acknowledged that the full Terafab cost is not yet incorporated into the company's 2026 capital expenditure plan, which already exceeds $20 billion. No construction timeline was provided at the launch event, [Electrek noted](https://electrek.co/2026/03/22/tesla-spacex-terafab-chip-factory-ai-desperation/). It remains unclear how costs will be divided among the three participating entities.

## Industry Context

The announcement arrives amid a period of sustained capacity pressure in the semiconductor industry. TSMC, which controls roughly 70 percent of the global foundry market, spent $165 billion developing six Arizona fabs, with 2-nanometer production there not expected to reach commercial output until 2029. A single 2-nanometer fabrication facility typically costs approximately $28 billion and requires 38 months to build in the United States, [Electrek reported](https://electrek.co/2026/03/22/tesla-spacex-terafab-chip-factory-ai-desperation/).

Neither Tesla nor SpaceX has prior experience in semiconductor fabrication. [TechCrunch noted](https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/22/elon-musk-unveils-chip-manufacturing-plans-for-spacex-and-tesla/) that Musk has a history of announcing large-scale manufacturing ventures that face extended timelines, pointing to the 4680 battery cell program announced in September 2020 as a precedent where production targets were significantly revised downward.

Tesla currently sources its AI inference chips through partnerships with Samsung and other suppliers including TSMC and Micron Technology. In January 2026, Tesla announced a $2 billion investment in xAI with a broader collaboration framework, a precursor to the vertically integrated approach that Terafab represents.
